title: Unit Final: Financial Decision-Making and Goal Setting

questions.prompt: What does opportunity cost help explain in a financial decision?

choices.text: What is given up by choosing one option over another
choices.feedback: Correct. Opportunity cost helps explain the next best option that was not chosen.

choices.text: The total money a person has earned
choices.feedback: Opportunity cost is about decision comparison, not total income.

choices.text: A bonus paid after saving money
choices.feedback: Opportunity cost is not a bonus or reward.

choices.text: Every possible cost in a full budget
choices.feedback: Opportunity cost focuses on the next best alternative, not every cost in a budget.

questions.prompt: Which statement best explains why needs usually come before wants?

choices.text: Needs are always more expensive
choices.feedback: Needs are not always more expensive than wants.

choices.text: Needs often support basic responsibilities or required expenses
choices.feedback: Correct. Needs usually come first because they often support important responsibilities or essential costs.

choices.text: Wants never have any value
choices.feedback: Wants can still have value, but they are not always the first financial priority.

choices.text: Wants should always be purchased last no matter what
choices.feedback: Needs usually come first, but financial decisions still depend on the specific situation.

questions.prompt: A strong financial goal should usually include both a target amount and a time frame.

choices.text: True
choices.feedback: Correct. A target amount and time frame make a financial goal more measurable.

choices.text: False
choices.feedback: A goal is usually stronger when it has a clear amount and a timeline.

questions.prompt: A student needs $210 in 7 months for a school program. How much should the student save each month to stay on target?

choices.feedback: Saving $25 for 7 months would only total $175.
Saving $35 each month would total too much for this calculation.
Saving $40 each month would total too much for this calculation.
Correct. $210 divided by 7 months equals $30 per month.

questions.prompt: A student has $80 and must choose between paying a $35 required activity fee, buying $40 shoes that are optional, and saving money for a future device repair. Which is the strongest first decision?

choices.text: Buy the shoes first because they cost the most
choices.feedback: The highest-cost option is not automatically the strongest priority.

choices.text: Pay the required activity fee first because it is a need and a current priority
choices.feedback: Correct. The required fee is the strongest first priority because it is a current need.

choices.text: Ignore the fee and save everything for the future repair
choices.feedback: A current required cost should usually be handled before an optional future decision.

choices.text: Spend the money quickly so no decision is needed
choices.feedback: Avoiding the decision does not strengthen financial reasoning.

questions.prompt: A student can buy a $22 concert shirt today or keep the $22 for a bus pass due next week. What is the strongest explanation of the opportunity cost of buying the shirt?

choices.text: The opportunity cost is the lost progress toward the bus pass payment
choices.feedback: This idea is related, but it does not fully account for the transportation need in the scenario.

choices.text: The opportunity cost is the shirt itself
choices.feedback: The opportunity cost is what is given up, not what is chosen.

choices.text: The opportunity cost is giving up money that could have supported a transportation need next week
choices.feedback: Correct. Buying the shirt means giving up money that could have gone toward the bus pass.

choices.text: There is no opportunity cost because both options are equal
choices.feedback: There is an opportunity cost whenever one option is chosen over another.

questions.prompt: Decision-making in personal finance should compare options, consequences, and future goals.

choices.text: False
choices.feedback: Strong financial decisions should compare multiple factors, including future goals.

choices.text: True
choices.feedback: Correct. Good financial decision-making compares options, consequences, and long-term goals.

questions.prompt: A student wants to save $160 in 4 months but keeps spending $20 each month on impulse purchases. What is the most likely result?

choices.text: The goal will be easier to reach
choices.feedback: Impulse spending usually makes it harder to reach a savings goal.

choices.text: The student will likely fall behind on the goal unless spending changes
choices.feedback: This is strong reasoning, but the correct answer in this set more directly connects the repeated spending to the missed target.

choices.text: The monthly target becomes less important
choices.feedback: The monthly target is still important if the goal is to be reached on time.

choices.text: The repeated impulse spending will reduce savings progress and can keep the student from reaching the target on time
choices.feedback: Correct. Impulse spending can slow progress and weaken the plan.

questions.prompt: Which decision strategy is strongest when two options both seem useful?

choices.text: Choose the option that sounds more exciting without comparison
choices.feedback: Excitement alone is not enough for a strong financial decision.

choices.text: Ignore long-term impact and focus only on today
choices.feedback: Long-term impact is a key part of financial reasoning.

choices.text: Compare cost, value, timing, and consequences before deciding
choices.feedback: Correct. Comparing several factors leads to stronger financial decisions.

choices.text: Choose quickly so the decision feels easier
choices.feedback: Quick decisions may ignore evidence and consequences.

questions.prompt: Why does Unit 1 connect financial decisions to goals and priorities?

choices.text: Because priorities and goals help a person judge whether a choice is worth its cost and consequence
choices.feedback: This idea is related, but it does not fully connect decisions, priorities, and long-term outcomes.

choices.text: Because goals and priorities make comparison unnecessary
choices.feedback: Goals and priorities support comparison; they do not replace it.

choices.text: Because financial decisions are strongest when they support important needs or goals instead of random spending
choices.feedback: Correct. Unit 1 emphasizes financial choices that are guided by priorities, goals, and reasoning.

choices.text: Because short-term enjoyment should always be the first priority
choices.feedback: Short-term enjoyment is not always the strongest priority in financial decision-making.
